topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed a relentless, irritating tune. Mark tapped his pen against the mahogany desk, the rhythmic clicking a sharp counterpoint to the silence he desperately craved. He’d been waiting for this promotion for eighteen months, and every day felt like wading through thick mud. When Sarah presented the Q3 projections, highlighting her impressive metrics, a cold knot tightened in Mark’s stomach. He glanced at the attached compensation sheet. The numbers swam before his eyes. Sarah’s salary was nearly double his. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in his chest. He forced a tight, professional smile, the muscles around his jaw flexing uncomfortably. He needed to process this, needed to figure out how to move forward, but the sheer disparity in their pay made his hands tremble slightly as he gripped his mouse.

[story 2]

"Just one more thing, Mark?" Sarah asked, leaning over his cubicle wall, her voice bright and entirely unconcerned. She slid a printed document across the partition. Mark picked it up, his fingers brushing against the crisp paper, and felt a sudden, sharp jolt of frustration. He scanned the figures detailing departmental bonuses. The gap between his current earnings and hers was staggering. He felt a tightness around his throat, a need to physically push away the desk. He cleared his throat, the sound rougher than intended. "Right. Let's review this," he managed, his tone deliberately flat, trying to keep the tremor out of his voice. He felt a distinct urge to stand up, to pace, to do anything other than stare at the stark reality laid out before him.

[story 3]

I watched the clock tick past two o'clock. Every second stretched into an agonizing eternity. When the HR email finally arrived detailing the salary structure adjustments, my breath hitched. The figures for the junior analyst, Ben, were astronomical compared to mine. A strange, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I slammed the file shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet corner of the office. I stood up abruptly, the cheap plastic chair scraping against the linoleum floor. I needed air. I needed distance. I walked towards the window, staring out at the indifferent city traffic, my knuckles white where I clenched my fists. The feeling inside was a tight, coiled spring, ready to snap.
